other_observers: Many other birders present waiting for and watching the Brambling while I was present, including: Morgan Churchill, Zachary Gayk, Christopher Wood, Jessie Barry, and Paul Hurtado.; Originally found by Mike Henwood on 19-November-2012.
duration: Seen for about 30 seconds to 1 minute per sighting, totaling to about 5 minutes of total observation
specific_location: Bear Creek Lake Regional Park: 39.654421 N, 105.149664 W
description: Smallish, sparrow/finch-like bird. About the same size as nearby House Finches (Carpodacus mexicanus) and American Tree Sparrows (Spizella arborea). Mostly gray head and nape, with black flecking/feathering around edge of auriculars and on crown. White partial eye-ring. Throat, breast, and "shoulder" tawny-orange. Mostly tawny-orange wingbar, with otherwise black wing. White belly. Back with black feathers edged extensively with tawny orange. Pale tawny color on sides with some black markings. Mostly black tail, with white rump (seen only briefly in flight). Mostly yellow bill.Behaviors: Seen briefly 4 times over the course of 4-5 hours. It would come down from the bushes and feed on seed on the ground that had been put out for the Bramblilng (and House Finches and sparrows it was associating with). Very flighty, and would only remain on the ground in the open feeding for about a minute at a time. It would disappear into the nearby bushes for 20 minutes to an hour at a time.Call: Not heard.Plumage: First-fall (basic plumage)
